resources.string: Open the Quiet Grid GitHub repository
You already have an active puzzle. You can continue it now or replace it with a new puzzle.
Choose a puzzle and keep your active run close.
These games are still in testing and may have rough edges.
Choose your theme, language, and timer display.
Language texts were AI-generated and may contain small mistakes.
Keeps the board and menus in a darker palette.
Uses a brighter palette across the app.
Uses a paper-like grayscale palette across the app.
Shows the elapsed timer while a puzzle is in progress.
Unlocks games that are still in testing. These may have rough edges.
Shows the next valid move on the board.
Ends the current puzzle without a solution.
This puzzle type does not include a tutorial yet.
Your progress will not be saved and the puzzle will end.
You set a new best score on this difficulty.
You recorded your first solved score on this difficulty.
All statistics stay on this device. No account required. No data is sent anywhere.
Remove all saved statistics and the current active puzzle? This cannot be undone.
Small, practical help for questions, feedback, and trust details.
Quiet Grid works fully offline. No data leaves your device.
Quiet Grid does not collect, transmit, sell, or share personal data. The app does not require an account and does not send data to us or to third parties.
Quiet Grid stores a small amount of app data locally so the app can work as expected. This includes theme preference, local statistics, and active puzzle progress.
Quiet Grid does not use ads, analytics, tracking, crash reporting services, or third-party online services.
Quiet Grid is a privacy-first puzzle app built for calm focus.
Quiet Grid keeps logic puzzles lightweight, fully offline, ad-free, and free to use. The goal is simple: clear rules, supportive pacing, and no distractions.
Quiet Grid currently includes several puzzle games, with local statistics, dark and light themes, and no accounts.
Quiet Grid source code is available under the GNU General Public License v3.0.
Quiet Grid also uses open-source packages that keep their own licenses. You can review the full source code and package list in the project repository.
Tap the numbers in order before they disappear.
Tap every number in ascending order before the earlier ones disappear from view.
Tap the next number in sequence. Each correctly tapped number disappears immediately, so you must remember where the remaining ones are.
Tapping any cell that is not the next number in sequence ends the puzzle immediately.
Numbers appear at random positions each round. Scan the whole grid before tapping.
Always tap 1 first, then 2, 3, and so on. There is no time pressure while the numbers are visible.
Each correct tap removes that number from the grid. You must recall the positions of the remaining numbers from memory.
Each successful round adds one more number. Reach the maximum count to solve the puzzle.
Take a moment to trace a path through all numbers before tapping 1. A quick mental route is faster than searching mid-sequence.
Notice clusters of consecutive numbers. Tapping neighbours one after another is faster than jumping across the grid.
Score is based on total time across all rounds. Faster tapping earns a higher score.
You ended this puzzle before it was solved.
Fill grid with 0s and 1s using logic.
More starting cells and gentler deductions.
Balanced openings that ask you to read the grid a bit further ahead.
Tighter setups with less free information and stronger pattern work.
Sparse openings with sustained deduction pressure across the whole puzzle.
Fill every cell with a 0 or 1 following three rules.
Tap any cell to cycle through empty → 0 → 1 → empty.
Completing a row or column that breaks a rule flashes it and costs 500 points.
Three identical digits in a row or column are not allowed.
Every row and column must contain the same number of 0s and 1s.
Every row is different from every other row, and every column from every other column.
Two matching digits next to each other force the cells on both sides to be the opposite digit.
When the same digit appears on both sides of an empty cell, that empty cell must be the opposite.
Once a row or column has as many 0s (or 1s) as it can hold, all remaining cells must be the other digit.
A row or column cannot duplicate a completed one. If it would, use the opposite value.
If one value would eventually force three in a row, the other value must go here.
This part of the puzzle does not offer a strong next move right now. Try another row or column, then ask again.
Place %1$d in the highlighted cell. Why: two %2$ds already sit together in %3$s, so another %2$d would create three in a row.
Place %1$d in the highlighted cell. Why: %2$s already shows %3$d _ %3$d, so the open cell between them must be %1$d to avoid three in a row.
Place %1$d in the highlighted cell. Why: %2$s already has %3$d %4$d%5$s, so the remaining open cell must be %1$d to keep the line balanced.
Place %1$d in the highlighted cell. Why: if %2$s matched %3$s, the completed %4$s would stop being unique.
Change one highlighted cell in %1$s. Why: three %2$ds in a row break the no-trios rule.
Change one highlighted cell in %1$s. Why: %1$s already contains %2$d %3$d%4$s, but limit is %5$d.
Change one highlighted cell. Why: %2$s and %3$s match, but completed %1$s must stay unique.
Starts at 10,000 and drops while the timer runs. Each incorrectly completed line costs 500 points. Score drops more slowly on harder difficulties.
When you correctly complete a row or column, all its cells briefly flash as confirmation.
You ended this puzzle before it was solved.
Fill cells to match the row and column clues.
Small clue patterns with clear openings.
Balanced grids that need steady deduction.
Tighter clue sets that require deeper overlap checks.
Dense grids with less obvious forced moves.
Fill cells to form the exact blocks described by each row and column clue.
Tap a cell to cycle: blank → filled → empty. Swipe across cells to paint them all with the same state.
If a row or column can no longer match its clues, it is marked as invalid.
Clue numbers list block lengths from left to right (or top to bottom), with at least one gap between each block.
If every valid placement for a block covers the same cell, that cell must be filled.
Cells that cannot belong to any valid block placement must stay empty.
Once a line is complete, the remaining cells in that line must stay empty.
Slide a block between its leftmost and rightmost valid position — any cell covered in both extremes must be filled.
If no block can legally reach a cell in any valid arrangement, mark it empty.
Once all blocks in a line are fully placed, every remaining cell in that line must be empty.
%1$s can no longer match its clues.
Mark the 1 cell in %1$s as empty.
Mark the %2$d cells in %1$s as empty.
Finish %1$s by marking the 1 remaining cell empty.
Finish %1$s by marking the %2$d remaining cells empty.
Score starts at 10,000 and drops as the timer runs. Solving faster earns a higher score.
You ended this puzzle before it was solved.
More breathing room for early scanning and steady clue reading.
A balanced board with more mines and fewer safe openings.
Tighter spaces that reward careful flagging and clue tracking.
Dense minefields with very little breathing room from the start.
Reveal every safe cell without opening a mine.
Tap to reveal a cell. Long-press to place or remove a flag.
Opening a mine ends the puzzle immediately.
Tap any hidden cell you think is safe to open it.
Each revealed number shows how many mines touch that cell, including diagonals.
Long-press any hidden cell to mark or unmark it as a suspected mine.
The puzzle is complete once every safe cell is revealed.
When a clue\'s remaining hidden neighbors equal its mine count, every one of them must be a mine — flag them all.
When a clue already has all its mines flagged, every remaining hidden neighbor is safe to reveal.
Two clues that share hidden cells can together reveal more than either clue alone — the overlap exposes which cells are safe and which are mines.
No clue points to a certain safe reveal right now. This spot may need a guess, so trust your best read of the board and ask again after the next reveal.
Safe next move from comparing clues
Reveal highlighted %1$s. This local clue pattern still leaves one mine slot, which makes the other hidden %1$s safe.
Reveal highlighted %1$s. Around %2$s, known mine positions already account for all %3$d %4$s.
Reveal highlighted %1$s. This clue\'s mine requirement is fully resolved, so its remaining hidden %1$s are safe.
Reveal highlighted %1$s. Reading %2$s together with %3$s leaves only one legal place for the remaining mine.
Reveal highlighted %1$s. If this tile were a mine, nearby clue counts would be too high.
Starts at 10,000 and drops while the timer runs. Finishing faster keeps more score.
Large empty openings reveal several safe cells at once and often expose the first strong clues.
When two revealed numbers touch some of the same hidden cells, compare their remaining mine counts before placing flags.
A flag is only as reliable as the reasoning behind it — don\'t treat flagged cells as confirmed unless the clues support it.
You ended this puzzle before it was solved.
This puzzle ended when a mine was opened. A fresh puzzle is ready when you want one.
Place digits 1 through 9 so every row, column, and box stays valid.
Starter grids meant for singles-first human solving.
Balanced grids that can grow into note-aware technique checks.
Sparse grids reserved for richer move analysis and validation work.
Very sparse grids reserved for advanced chain and coloring deductions.
This position may need a deeper technique than Sudoku currently teaches in Quiet Grid.
Digit %2$d appears more than once in %1$s. Correct that conflict before asking for the next move.
%1$s has no valid digit left. Correct the surrounding entries before asking for the next move.
Only digit %1$d fits %2$s after checking its row, column, and box.
Digit %1$d only fits one cell in %2$s, so place it in %3$s.
Digits %1$s are locked inside %2$s, so remove them from the other highlighted cells in %3$s.
%1$s removes %2$s from %3$s. The highlighted cells leave those candidates no valid home there.
Place each digit 1–9 exactly once in every row, column, and 3×3 box.
Tap a cell to select it, then tap a digit button to place it. Toggle Notes mode to pencil in candidates instead.
A duplicate digit in the same row, column, or box is highlighted as a conflict.
No digit may appear twice in the same row, column, or box.
The pre-filled digits are fixed — you cannot change them.
Use notes when a cell is not ready
Toggle Notes mode to pencil in possible digits for a cell and cross them out as the puzzle narrows down.
When only one digit is allowed in a cell after ruling out every digit already in its row, column, and box, place it.
When a digit can only fit one cell within a row, column, or box, place it there even if other candidates are still visible in that cell.
Write all possible digits as notes, then cross them out as surrounding rows, columns, and boxes fill in — until only one is left.
Starts at 10,000 and drops while the timer runs. Each incorrectly completed row, column, or box costs 500 points. Score drops more slowly on harder difficulties.
Scan one house at a time
Pick one row, one column, or one box and ask which digits are still missing. Small, local checks stay easier to trust than broad guesses.
If a cell still has multiple valid digits, switch to Notes mode instead of guessing. Pencilled candidates make hidden singles and other forced placements much easier to spot.
You ended this Sudoku session before solving the grid.
Trace listed words in straight lines and solve the hidden bonus word from the grid.
Horizontal and vertical forward words with generous spacing.
Adds diagonal forward words and tighter clusters.
All directions including backwards with denser overlaps.
Largest grids with full-direction words and heavy overlap.
Find all the words to unlock the hidden word.
The hidden-word letters sit in the empty cells, left to right then top to bottom. Tap them in order.
A wrong hidden-word tap resets your progress.
Start from the highlighted letter and trace the full word in one straight line.
The highlighted cell is the next hidden-word letter in order.
Find every listed word hidden in the grid, then solve the hidden bonus word to solve the puzzle.
Tap the first letter of a word, then tap the last letter to select. Words run in straight lines: horizontal, vertical, or diagonal.
Invalid selections are silently ignored — there is no penalty for tracing wrong paths.
Every listed word appears in one straight line: horizontal, vertical, or diagonal depending on difficulty.
Invalid selections are ignored, so you can keep scanning without losing the run.
The hidden-word letters fill the empty cells in reading order — left to right, top to bottom. Once all words are found, tap each letter in sequence to win.
Find uncommon initials in the grid first, then expand in all allowed directions.
Commit one line at a time
Begin a selection, drag to the endpoint, and commit. Starting a new selection replaces the temporary one.
You ended this Word Search session before solving every listed word and the hidden word.
